data.title: The Chefs' Warehouse: Sold Long Ago, Now It's Dangerously Overvalued
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

In this article, I'll give you an update on The Chefs' Warehouse (NASDAQ:CHEF), a company I reviewed long ago. In fact, over 1.5 years ago, and missed out on updating it in time to really make a difference. If you look at my article on the company that you will find here, you will note that despite giving the company a PT of $33/share, I considered the company a "HOLD" in my article when it was trading at $28/share.

I simply did not believe the company to be cheap enough. I liked the business - and the margins, and the products, and the growth. I still do. I just don't/didn't like the price.

However, if you note, the company actually dropped almost directly after my article, going down below $20/share. So I bought stock.

But this is not a victory lap for a few reasons. First off, it was a very small position. Not exactly high in conviction - less than 0.15% of my total. Secondly, I already sold it in 2023 when the company recovered, and I could make a sweet 30-40% RoR on that sale. Third, and most important, I didn't provide you, my readers, with an update for when the stock was actually cheap. Granted, I made the targets clear in my article, but I should have, at the very least, provided a commentary update.

I didn't.

I plan to change that now.

My objective here is to show you that the company is only attractive If you forecast at a premium, upgrade the PT, and show you where I would buy Chef's warehouse in 2025.

Chef's Warehouse - a company with an attractive business idea and plans.

I, in fact, love specialty distributors. Moats and hard-to-enter markets are attractive, and specialty distributors like this with global operations, which this company has, are attractive. Chef's Warehouse still works with restaurants, fine dining, and various end sales points and stores to deliver over 70,000 different products to market. It works not only with standard food, but works with specialty food such as very fine cheeses, meats, caviars and the like, much of it hormone-free.

You get the picture - and it's been around for a long time. It's not even close to "new", with roots going back to 1985. The company's distribution centers are global. As I mentioned/implied in my last article, this is the sort of company I love to own. Its business is in something I can "get into". That's also why I bought it as soon as it dropped to anywhere remotely cheap.

Even though the company lacked, and in fact still lacks, a dividend. This is one of the major hurdles. This company is as old as me and still lacks a dividend. With the margins, it has and the earnings it generates, I believe this is one of the largest mistakes made by the business - and I do punish the company for this in my price target calculations.

Excepting something like COVID-19, the demands for this company's products are timeless. Luxury eating? It will always be "on the menu" if you pardon the pun.

The negatives for the business are easy to summarize. It's a low margin industry with easily spoiled and low-shelf-life products, which also is exposed to high amounts of inflation. The company also uses debt more liberally than it should, which is why it's only B+ rated with a 60% long-term debt to cap. It has a leverage of 3.1x, and this means that combined with its end markets, there's this risk. There's a reason many of the food suppliers and grocers run a much "cleaner" balance sheet in terms of debt. You want that flexibility - at least, that's my view.

Aside from this? Not many risks.

The upsides are equally clear. Synergies from M&A's and other growth to make this company more efficient are massive. Scale advantages here are nothing to sneeze at, and indeed, the company is targeting a margin improvement above 6.5% in terms of EBITDA for the next few years. Also, revenue growth has been gang busters and is expected to continue along this path, 15% or above in terms of growth. Digital advancements are also no joke here. The company is also updating sourcing and logistics, really making it more efficient with the growth happening here.

As I said, this is a company that I wish I could own. I wish I could buy stock, and I regret selling when I did - but to be clear, if I still owned it, I would sell the company here, 100%. The company's capital allocation priorities are my main issue - not because the company invests, which is what they should do, and the leverage range down to a 2-3x range (though 3x is still too high in my book) is fine, it's that there's not a single word on how to reward shareholders with the exception of share buybacks. Some of that projected growth and adjusted EBITDA for 2025 should, in my view, go to shareholder pockets.

But my wishful thinking won't change things.

Q4'24 and FY24 were good periods. The company saw growth in every single segment and market, with overall sales up almost 9%, which is a remarkable performance given the market we are in. The company is also making significant efficiency gains across the board. EBITDA per employee is a good way to measure this fact.

Online orders continue to grow, with now 56%+ of specialty location customers ordering online, which obviously increases company efficiency. The company's financial goals are clear, and I believe them to be realistic.

Frankly, if the company was cheap, there would be no question. We could overcome those metrics and drawbacks and buy shares, and be happy with the capital appreciation as well as the buybacks. But the thing is, despite buybacks, CHEF has been quite dilutive in terms of shares since 2017, increasing from around 25.92M to almost 38M.

I say that not because it's an issue that they dilute, I say it's because I have an issue with how they dilute. Specifically, the SBC that's being recorded. Please note the trends below.

My issue is that while management is busy rewarding themselves, shareholders are being rewarded through dilution, and buybacks are barely keeping track with these SBC trends. To be clear, the amount of SBC for the year 2024 is more than 35% of the company's net income for the fiscal of the same year. This is the reason I am highlighting the lack of a dividend, the fact that I want to own this company but cannot, and why I would sell any shares I held at this particular time.

Chef's Warehouse - I'd need to see a much cheaper price before going in

Obviously, given the return trends since my purchase (if I'd held) or my last article, my complaints about SBC and other things might ring hollow. But keep in mind that you also need to look at what could happen going forward. As I mentioned, the current buyback trends are not enough to keep dilution from happening. The company quite recently peaked in February and has dropped down since then.

I do not view premiumization of this company as valid at a high level because, first, of the lack of a dividend and, second, the capital allocation strategy when taking into account the company's SBC trends.

I do not view close to $20M in SBC as valid when that's almost 10% of your company's total EBITDA - at least not without rewarding shareholders as well, outside of the C-suite. In order for that to make sense, I want to be able to buy shares at 10-13x P/E, no higher, and even with the growth rate we have here, no higher than 15-18x.

That's not what we have here. CHEF currently trades at 32x P/E. That means that in order to get 15% or above annualized, we need to expect the company to continue trading alongside such trends. At 32x P/E forward, we get 18.45% per year.

The 3-5 year average comes to around 25-26x P/E. Even forecasted at this level, your upside would be no more than 9.5% per year, so less than double digits - and this is still high for a company that does not have a good history of hitting targets set - in fact, more often than not miss its targets by quite a wide margin.

While I argue that the company lacks any sort of direct or comparable peers, unless you go for your more "standard food distributors/suppliers", what we see here is, in my view, insufficient to argue for or justify a high fair-value multiple. I went through in my last article that such a high multiple would have to justify the risk we see for expansion CapEx, high costs, inflation, labor costs, and digitization costs. Add to that now the increased SBC as well as the fact of tariffs, which might impact all the company's US operations, depending on sourcing and logistics.

The lack of a dividend, combined with these facts, means that I am unwilling to take these risks here, given the company's current forecast and accuracy profile. I would take my previous share price target of around $33/share and raise this, in light of increased earnings and upside, to about $40/share. This comes to about 20x P/E for the 2026-2027E period, which I would consider fair. However, this also means that we want to buy the company below this.

General price targets for the company, looking at analysts, come to between $68-$73/share with an average of almost $70/share (Source: Paywalled TIKR.com link). That sounds to me like those analysts aren't taking into consideration the SBC or the ongoing dilution, or what the lack of dividends means here. Even a 27- 28x P/E doesn't go all the way up to $70/share.

Overall, I agree with Seeking Alpha's Quant system grading on the valuation here - it's not good, for the reasons I said, but also because of the lower peer sector median of 18x P/E. Note that I am valuing the company close to this. The closest peer would be SpartanNash (SPTN), a company I also cover here on Seeking Alpha - but that one is far cheaper and also pays a dividend to investors. SPTN is actually a company that, while in trouble, I would consider a bit more attractive here than CHEF at this valuation and fundamentals.

The fact is, it's been a long time since I've reviewed a company that I wanted to invest in so much, but that based on fundamentals and valuation and shareholder renumeration, I cannot at this time.

Nonetheless, the overall thesis for the company as of 2025 and April is as follows.

Thesis

  • Chefs' Warehouse is an interesting business with a working business model, but an overall very low margin of below 1.5% net. This necessitates conservative estimates when investing in the business to not risk negative development in a downturn through investing in over-premiumized equity - as we've seen in the last few months.
  • I would be interested in buying Chefs' Warehouse when we get more visibility from the EMEA sector and a clearer view of costs for this year.
  • I give Chefs' Warehouse a PT of $33/share but still rate the company a "HOLD" in the next few months for the reasons mentioned here and in the article.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of €140/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2025.

data.title: Fed Faces Difficult Balancing Act In A Global Trade War
data.description:
Is inflation or slower growth, and perhaps recession, the priority? This basic question hangs over the Federal Reserve as it grapples with deciding how to respond to global trade war.

The risks shot higher after the US imposed higher tariffs on its major trading partners today, led by 104% import taxes on Chinese goods. Countries around the world are retaliating with their own tariffs, while sending envoys to Washington in the hope of negotiating better terms. Where all this leads is anyone’s guess, but for now at least, it surely puts the Fed in an unusually uncomfortable spot of trying to set policy for a rapidly changing macro environment.

Using the policy-sensitive US 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) as a guide suggests the pressure for a rate cut is building. The 2-year yield fell to 3.74% on Tuesday (April 8), close to the lowest level since September. As a result, the 2-year yield has fallen well below the current median effective Fed funds target rate of 4.33%, which suggests that the market is betting on rate cuts.

Fed funds futures are increasingly pricing in a higher chance for a rate cut at the next FOMC meeting on May 7. The current implied probability that the central bank will ease rates is 53%, based on CME data.

The assumption is that a global trade war will curtail economic activity. But there’s also concern that sharply higher tariffs will raise inflation. The key problem for the Fed is that it’s difficult, if not impossible, to address both challenges simultaneously. Monetary policy, in short, is not designed to work well in a stagflation scenario.

That leaves the Fed with the task of choosing whether to prioritize inflation taming or supporting growth.

“They are in a no-win situation,” said Laurence Meyer, a former Fed governor, in a Wall Street Journal story today. Another source for the article explains:

“This administration has generated the worst shock possible for the Fed, and there’s nothing that they can do right now,” observed Riccardo Trezzi, a former Fed economist who runs Geneva-based Underlying Inflation, a consulting firm. “Maybe they will get lucky, and they choose one of the two sides of the mandate, and ex post it will turn out they did the right thing.”

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Target: Tariff Fears Overblown (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Target (NYSE:TGT), I downgraded the stock to a “hold,” as the company saw a deceleration in the pace of its comparable sales growth, while its profit margins deteriorated from expanding inventory levels. Plus, the impact of President Trump’s proposed tariffs on imports to the US was highly unknown at the time.

Since the time of my previous publication, the stock has hugely underperformed the S&P 500 index, declining over 27%.

Target reported its Q4 FY24 earnings in early March, where both revenue and earnings per share came above estimates. However, the management warned of an uncertain consumer demand landscape amid the current tariff environment as well as margin pressures that it is likely to face in the first half of the year.

However, the management reiterated their confidence in generating an incremental $15B in revenue growth over the next five years as it makes strategic investments in stores and digital, product assortments, customer experience, and supply chain management.

With analysts slashing their forward revenue and earnings estimates for the company, sentiment has hit a trough, as the stock becomes the most oversold in all of the S&P 500.

While it is unknown as to whether the downward revisions to the top and bottom lines have bottomed, I believe the extreme negative pessimism has created an attractive “buy” opportunity from a risk-reward perspective.

In this post, I have conducted two separate valuation exercises, where I have determined the price target based on a 50:50 probability of either the base or the bear case scenario playing out.

As a result, I will rate the stock a “buy” with a price target of $115, representing an upside of 31%.

A quick summary of Q4 FY24 earnings

Target reported its Q4 FY24 earnings in early March, where revenue declined 0.8% YoY to $30.9B, exceeding estimates. During the quarter, comparable sales grew 1.5%, at a faster pace than the previous quarter.

However, when we look deeper into the data, we can see that Store-Originated comparable sales continued to decline, albeit at a slower pace than the previous quarter, which was offset by a robust growth rate of Digital comparable sales at 8.7%.

In terms of profitability, Target reported non-GAAP EPS (earnings per share) of $2.41, which came in higher than expected. However, when we look at gross and operating profit, we can see that it declined 4.4% and 21% YoY, respectively, given slower topline growth amid weak consumer spending.

Plus, inventory levels also expanded by over 7% YoY, which was a combination of receipt timing volatility and pulling forward receipts to introduce greater newness in categories such as apparel and hard lines. At the same time, they made additional investments to improve product availability as they added two new food distribution centers.

On the topic of inventory, the management emphasized shorter lead times to help them stay better aligned with the latest trends and reduce inventory risk. Simultaneously, the management also discussed that they are investing in AI to modernize their inventory management systems, with the new systems now used by about 40% of the assortment, double that of 2023, thus allowing for more accurate forecasting, efficiency gains, and cost reduction.

Looking forward, the management guided for revenue to grow in a range of around 1% in FY25, with a “modest increase” in operating margin on a year-over-year basis, translating to an Adjusted EPS of roughly $9.3 per share.

During the earnings call, the management outlined that they expect to generate an additional $15B in revenue growth over the next five years, with particular optimism towards categories that include beauty, apparel, home, and areas of hard lines that include books and toys, as they invest in new stores and remodels, digital, the Target 360 loyalty program, same-day delivery, and partnerships through Target Plus to engage their customers and drive higher purchase frequency.

The full impact of the tariffs is still unknown, but management is taking necessary precautions.

However, the biggest fear that is probably weighing in on investor confidence is the impact of tariffs. Particularly, when it comes to the profit margin, the management expects pressures in Q1 FY25 and in the first half of the year, driven by tariff uncertainty as well as a ramp-up in new stores, remodel projects, and the timing of SG&A and tax expenses.

Yet, the management also reiterated that they have managed through tariffs in the past, and given their experienced team, both in sourcing and design, the management is confident regarding their diversification strategies pertaining to the country of production, thus providing room for more flexibility.

“We have a really incredible team, both in global sourcing as well as design, decades of experience; and under their leadership, they've been very proactive and been thinking about this for years now and have been working to diversify our country of production, and so we have been pulling--moving things out of China to other places around the world, looking at going across Asia as well as western hemisphere - Guatemala, Honduras, so we have a much more diverse set of countries from which we produce. I think that's just going to give us more flexibility and help us be more agile.”

Plus, the management is heavily focused on shortening lead times for their inventory to reduce volatility while remaining committed to their Capex of $4-5B which will be directed at stores, both existing and new, along with investments in supply chain capabilities.

But, at the end of the day, there are many unknown factors here, the most notable of which is the impact on potential consumer demand, which could disproportionately hurt top-line growth. Although Target has been actively “scenario planning” to minimize the impact of tariffs and avoid price hikes for consumers, there is elevated uncertainty at the moment.

This has led to analysts slashing their forward estimates for both revenue and earnings growth, as can be seen below.

Target is now one of the most oversold stocks in the S&P 500 index. However, the bigger question is whether the downward revision to the company’s top and bottom lines has bottomed.

Honestly speaking, that remains to be seen, though it is possible that earnings may actually hold up better than expected, especially given the investments around boosting efficiencies around supply chain systems as well as in GenAI to elevate customer experience, thus providing additional operating leverage.

But, when it comes to investing, one of the important factors to consider is “risk-reward”. While it is possible that the downward revisions have not bottomed yet, I believe the tsunami of negative sentiment has created a rare “buy” opportunity for Target, which I will explain below.

Revisiting my valuation

Looking forward, I will conduct two valuation scenarios.

The first one will be based on the assumptions in the current base case by consensus estimates, where revenue is expected to grow 1.3% YoY in FY25 to $107.9B. After that, assuming that the management is able to deliver on growing the company’s revenue by $15B over the next three years, it would require a compounded annual growth rate of 3.0% between FY26 and FY29. Assuming that is the case, Target should grow its revenues to approximately $121.5B by FY29.

From a profitability standpoint, I will take forward consensus estimates for earnings per share under account over the next years, amounting to $13.04 per share by FY29, which will be equivalent to a present value of $8.91 when discounted at 10%.

Assuming that Target trades at par with the 10-year average price-to-earnings multiple of the S&P 500, where its companies grow their earnings by 8% every year, we arrive at a PE ratio of 15, or a price target of $133, representing an upside of 52% from its current levels.

Moving on to the second scenario, Target sees its revenues and earnings per share estimate for FY25 slashed by 7% as a result of inflation, which is induced by tariff-related pressures on the macroeconomic landscape. In this scenario, Target would likely generate a revenue of $99.1B with an earnings per share of $8.23. Assuming that tariff-related pressures last a year, before comps normalize and Target goes back to growing its revenues in the 3%, it will end FY29 with a total revenue of $111.5B, far lower than the base case estimate.

In terms of profitability, Target sees its earnings revert back to growing at par with current consensus estimates from FY26 onwards, resulting in $11.79 in earnings per share by FY29, which will be equivalent to a present value of $8.06 when discounted at 10%.

Assuming a lower PE ratio of 12, which is roughly 20% lower than the 10-year average price-to-earnings multiple of the S&P 500, we arrive at a price target of $96.7, representing an upside of 10% from its current levels.

Assuming there is a 50:50 chance of either of these scenarios playing out, we arrive at a price target of $115.15, representing an upside of 31% from its current levels.

My final verdict and conclusions

Target investors haven’t had it easy thus far.

With the company management warning investors of an uncertain consumer demand landscape, along with potential margin pressures from tariffs, sentiment has collapsed, with analysts slashing their forward estimates for revenue and earnings growth.

In all of this, we don’t know whether downward revisions to earnings have bottomed, which will be dependent on the company’s Q1 earnings and forward guidance.

However, in all of this, the management has laid out a plan to grow its revenues by $15B over the next five years through a combination of investments in its stores (both existing and new), digital, loyalty, partner networks, and more to engage their customer base and drive higher purchase frequency and volume through newness in assortment.

At the same time, the management also reiterated that they have been investing in boosting supply chain efficiencies while shortening lead times on inventories and diversifying their production outside of China, which should all shield against the tariff blow to a certain degree.

Finally, I believe that investments are ultimately about “risk-reward.”

At the moment, based on my analysis of a base case and a bear case scenario, I can see that the risk-reward indeed looks quite attractive, pointing to the fact that the selling pressure may have gone just a little too far.

Therefore, after assessing both the “good” and the “bad,” I will upgrade my rating to a “buy” with a price target of $115, representing an upside of 31% from its current levels.

data.title: Allot Ltd.: SECaaS Won't Save The Whole Ship
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Allot Ltd. (NASDAQ:ALLT) is overvalued for what it offers investors, particularly in light of its poor visibility.

More specifically, the stock is richly priced at 45x my estimated forward free cash flow. Particularly, given that the business is hardly growing at all.

On the plus side, the business does hold a solid amount of cash, which should help support its operations.

But as a whole, there's just too poor a risk-reward here, so I'm downgrading this stock to a sell.

Rapid Recap

Back in February, I said,

This stock has recently shown in my inbox with several readers asking me for my views on the stock. For my part, I see it a bit like Alarm Technologies (ALAR), another small-cap investor favorite from 2024, which had a bad ending to its growth story.

With the benefit of hindsight, that call at the time was timely. And now I look to update my thesis, with a bearish rating.

Allot Ltd.'s Near-Term Prospects

Allot makes it easier for internet and mobile service providers (such as Verizon) to protect their customers from online threats without those customers needing to install or manage anything themselves.

Think of it like automatic cybersecurity built into your phone or home internet, provided straight from your network provider. Their main value is that they help these service providers give people safe internet by offering tools that detect and block cyber threats across devices.

Furthermore, 2024 ended up as a turnaround year, with Allot Ltd. becoming embedded with major telecom providers, such as Vodafone, and with those partnerships just getting started, there are big hopes for this small-cap company.

Given this background, let's now discuss its fundamentals.

Revenue Growth Rates Likely to Remain at Low Single Digits

This is Allot's guidance from its earnings call,

Looking ahead, we expect stable level of revenue from our smart product line during the coming year. While this product line long-term visibility is less predictable than our SECaaS product line, we have a solid pipeline in 2025 and we believe there is a potential for upside.

Basically, management wants investors to fully focus on its cybersecurity subscription business (SECaaS), and to mostly disregard the rest of its operations.

The problem here is that its SECaaS business makes up 20% of Allot's total revenues. And what you have here is that the other 80% of its business is far from stable.

In fact, if 2024 is anything to go by, Allot's Support & Maintenance business unit makes up the majority of its business, and that is showing no growth.

As a result, I estimate that in the coming year, that side of the business will not significantly increase the overall profitability of Allot. Something we'll delve into further next.

ALLT Stock Valuation -- 45x Forward Free Cash Flow

Allot Ltd. holds about $17 million of net cash on its balance sheet. This is a fair amount of cash, given that the business is already generating positive free cash flows. One could make the case that the business is indeed on steady footing.

Furthermore, its net cash balance reaches more than 9% of its market cap. If we were to build a bull case for this investment, we could start on this consideration. That being said, I'm not entirely convinced that this cash balance alone is enough to overturn the overall aspect that the stock is not cheaply priced.

With that in mind, let's press ahead. Allot ended 2024 by making less than $3 million of free cash flow. This is a significant improvement from the negative $32 million of free cash flow produced in 2023, but it does force the question of whether or not this level of free cash flow can support its valuation?

For my part, I believe that the stock is overvalued.

Even if we take on board management's comments for "improved profitability" in 2025, it's difficult to extrapolate these words too far, given all the near-term macro turbulence in the economy right now.

For the sake of our discussion, I've estimated that in the best-case scenario, Allot Ltd. makes $4 million of free cash flow in 2025. This would be an increase from 2025, of more than 30% y/y, but in actual fact, largely similar to the prior year.

And this, in my opinion, is the best-case scenario. If my estimate turns out to be roughly accurate, this means that the stock today is priced at 45x forward free cash flow. A figure that, I believe, is unjustified, given its lack of visibility into its future free cash flows, together with its lackluster growth rates.

Risk Factors to the Upside

Investors today are truly bearish. Anyone reading the news today will know this to be the fact. As a consequence, we have to acknowledge that a lot of bad news is already priced into this stock, and the market overall.

So, it only takes a little bit of good news for ALLT to find its footing. To be clear, finding its footing does not mean that this stock rallies back higher. Instead, finding its footing means that stocks go sideways for a period of time. But going sideways is massively better than dropping aggressively, as we've embraced in the past month.

The other consideration that could reignite the upside potential is that after years of struggle, 2024 marked a real turnaround, with profits increasing and the business turning free cash flow positive. On top of that, their cybersecurity subscription business (SECaaS) is booming, with big growth expected again in 2025.

If investors once again buy into this narrative, this could see the stock find its footing, which could mean that this stock offers investors a compelling risk-reward, at least relative to other areas of the market that appear to be embracing a risk-off sentiment, for instance, businesses heavily dependent on supply chains.

The Bottom Line

When I look at Allot Ltd.’s financials, I just don’t see a compelling reason to stay bullish. Sure, the company has a decent cash cushion and finally turned free cash flow positive in 2024—but at 45x my forward free cash flow estimate, it’s simply too expensive for a business that isn’t growing in any meaningful way.

With the bulk of its revenue showing little to no growth and its promising SECaaS line still a small slice of the pie, I just can't justify the current valuation. That’s why I’m staying bearish here.

data.title: Mastering Sector ETFs: Smarter Investing Beyond The S&P 500
data.description:
Get full access to Sungarden YARP Portfolio now!

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Daniel Snyder: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Content is offered for information purposes only. Unless stated otherwise, any and all individuals participating in the video are third parties that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body. Unless stated otherwise, the views or opinions expressed may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. The accuracy and completeness of content shared cannot be guaranteed. Seeking Alpha does not take account of your objectives or financial situation and does not offer any personalized investment advice. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker, US investment adviser, or investment bank.

Hey. Welcome back to ETF Spotlight. I'm Daniel Snyder. And today, we are joined once again by Rob Isbitts. You might know him from Sungarden Investment Publishing here on Seeking Alpha or the world of ETFs because he is the man that has been ingrained in it so long. But today, we're going deeper. We're going macro. We're going into sectors. We're looking at all of them to see what he has seen in the market right now from what the price is telling us.

So, Rob, thank you again for jumping on the program. Wanted to see if we could just kick things off with obviously, markets have dropped significantly since about mid-February. What are you seeing with this correction, or is this just a moment in time? Should investors be worried? What are your thoughts?

Rob Isbitts: Yeah. Great to be with you again. And let me tell you, I think there's a lot of stuff that says we used to say, is kind of coming home to roost. Okay. My whole approach to investing is based on the idea that everybody knows how to play offense. Not that many people know how to play defense because they are not taught. And ever since the pandemic, we've had this sort of deceptively easy ride. And that doesn't make me a bull or bear. You know, I'm just a realist. But I think that understanding markets as it's not just what should I buy now, it should be how can I defend while I'm also trying to grow my wealth. Otherwise, you may end up going years as people did earlier this century, where you kind of net out to nothing.

And part of that is the way the S&P 500 is structured and has been for a while because tech has run away with everything, and it's created a fairly deceptive market so that even now when you get pretty rough performance for tech and the Mag 7, but you also get, let's say, some green shoots, if you will, from the smaller parts of the S&P 500 sector wise, it almost gets lost because everybody's so focused on the broad index and on the Nasdaq and where can I make money now?

DS: You have a very strong point there. Right? You always ever been told for the majority of investors is just invest in the S&P 500, right? Put the money there. Do it whether it's through your retirement or your individual portfolio…

RI: Yeah.

DS: ..and ride the wave. And it's typically worked out based off of the rebalancing approach of market capitalization. Now we're in a period of time, as you had mentioned, the Mag 7 at the top that have been dominating the index, but rightfully so because their earnings continue to grow. Right? So why is it that if earnings continue to grow that investors are now getting shaky in their boots here and saying, well, maybe tech's not the place I want to be.

RI: They're all in sync. Okay? The numbers are too high no matter where you look. Does that mean they can't continue? No. Because a year ago, two years ago, they were a little bit high. Now they're historically high, whether you're talking about P/E ratio, smooth P/E ratio, price to sales. And you're starting to see all of a sudden maybe a crest in the other thing that matters, which is liquidity. I can at least say that the odds are moving toward the next, let's call it big return, 10% up, 10% down, 20% up, 20% down. They're getting more decidedly toward down, not up. And that's really the beginning of it for me. So, yeah, I mean, the -- it's great that the numbers are great, but the prices of stocks more than reflect that in almost every sector.

DS: Now what about the people that say, you know, you want to buy low, sell high, but there's also the people then in the momentum camp that say, well, I buy high and I'll sell higher. So what is it about the momentum trade that might be broken right now that people need to know about?

RI: I guess to put it in very sort of layman's terms, this market's been driving over the speed limit probably in the 80s, okay? And I don't know too many 85 mile an hour speed limits, not around here, maybe Autobahn or something. But it's now starting to crest, if you will. And it's either going to be the old pause that refreshes, or I think more likely, I would say probably if I were going to put a number on it, three to one in favor of, you know, the next move of significance across most sectors being down. And look, I mean, this is not new to me. I'm not reacting to this so much because when I did what a lot of analysts did on Seeking Alpha in December and I put out my forecast for the year, I'm not much into forecast, but my best guess was, that the S& P 500 could settle this year end around 4,800. That was very much in minority, and I have no idea if I'm going to be right. It doesn't affect my portfolio positioning. But it does make me very well aware that defense goes with the offense because when you can play offense and defense at the same time, it's much more a matter of expressing investing, and this is all the way at the sector levels as well as at the broad market level.

Okay. How invested am I? It's a matter of degree, not a binary choice. And I think a lot of investors right now, they think of every decision as binary, and that is a habit I would encourage them to break and break quickly.

DS: So offense, defense sounds a little bit like a barbell approach that you're taking here with this specific viewpoint. But let's talk about sectors. Right? Let's talk about what you're seeing on the charts. Let's see, you know, what sectors right now look attractive to you in this market environment, and what is that telling you?

RI: Energy looked the best, but the best of a bad bunch. And I'm just looking here. I mean, since the market peaked on February 19, the exact same day that it peaked in 2020, by the way. Pandemic versus whatever we have today, but there are more similarities in people, I think, give it credit for, except that it's not a pandemic, but the emotional factor. So anyway, energy is the only sector that's up since that time. But I think what's more interesting is that these smaller sectors, okay, there are five sectors in the S&P that make up all of 16%. The other six sectors make up 84%. That's your tech, et cetera, and they've kind of pulled up the market averages. I think relatively speaking, and this is relative because right now, none of them look great to me, but those smaller sectors in particular, it's funny, I just wrote about Pfizer, and I'm not talking about Pfizer here, but, it is the P and the F and the E. Okay. Just like the Pfizer symbol. Those are the three, let's say, relatively look best. XLP is consumer staples. XLF is financials, although I question that one, even myself. Again, I think it's a very skittish market. And then the third one is energy, which still looks like a relative outperformer, but a lot of this is going to have to do with interest rates. And to me, the one other thing that people should keep in mind look. It's been very anti tech for a while. I think people are dramatically underestimating just how cheap something can get when they -- when they're looking to buy the dip.

DS: I would like to go back a second and double click on – you’d mentioned financials and how you don't feel like that one even though your - you said your data is showing you that financials is still kind of in this group. Right? Why do you feel, as an investor yourself, that financials are not going to be able to navigate this environment, especially with the market price and then the next Fed cut potentially being in June?

RI: Yeah. It sounds cliche, but that's a really good question to follow-up. The thing about financials to me, I look at everything as ABL, avoid big loss. So the very first thing I want to do is I want to manage risk. And then, you go from a philosophy to a process all the way down to your tactics, but risk management underlines it all. And when I look at any investment, so let's take these sectors. Okay? If I were to say, all right, which ones would I own if I were going to own -- if I had to own three or four? Okay. I guess financials would be in there if I had to own some of them. Now keep in mind, that's not going to move the S&P much because most of these sectors are small. Financials is bigger. But the issue with financials is the following, Daniel. We all remember 2008, and we also remember 2007. And one of the things that was common to probably the last four or five market declines, if that's what this is, one of magnitude, not just a correction, the thing about the financial sector is, they're on the hook for a lot of stuff. There's been a lot of margin, a lot of leverage, a lot of speculation. And to me, I always get the heebie-jeebies about financials, not my favorite sector by far. Okay?

And it's because there is that blow up risk that maybe doesn't exist the same way in the staples and utilities, which are regulated companies, even in tech, because as you said, the earnings and the balance sheets for a lot of the bigger companies are rock solid. The problem is the prices have just gone on the stratosphere. And I guess the other thing would be and this ties in financials and tech. Financials is kind of the big value sector, tech the big growth sector.

DS: I'd love to wrap things up here by asking you about I know we've covered a lot of the different sectors, and we look at the ETFs, right, that track them. But specifically, we think about what the administration is trying to do here. Right? They tell us that they want to bring money back to America. They want to reinvest in America. They want to build out America for the next stage. So what's the story with industrials? Where is it a constant, like, just waiting for the bottom and don't catch the falling knife? Or what's your take on the industrial sector?

RI: If you're in a chair and it's about to fall over and you catch yourself, but then you kind of lose track of what you're doing and you fall over onto the ground anyway, that's what a lot of this market looks like, and I think maybe we'll include the chart on the XLI for this. But the best thing I can say about XLI and why it is different from all the other sectors to me and why it is one of my favorite sectors pound for pound. It's the first place -- one of the first places I look for value at the stock level is the way it's structured, okay? The way the weightings are. If you look at the other 11 sectors – the other 10 sectors of the 11 in the S&P 500, they're all heavily weighted. Okay? Tech is three companies, Apple, Microsoft, Nvidia is like 40%-something of it or 30%-some. It's a lot. And they're all like that except for industrials. And I think this is just because they've never had sort of, like this major boom over the last decade. They've been a little steadier. And as a result, you've got a bunch of stocks in the XLI that are roughly the same market cap, and that's what all these things are based on, at least the popular ones. So because of that, I guess I would call it more like a mile wide and inch deep. You got more companies to choose from. So the sector may see the winners and the losers even out a bit in this environment. But my guess is that over time, there will be some pretty good winners that will come from there. And I thought this about a year ago when I first started running Sungarden YARP Portfolio when it was a portfolio before it was an investing group here. And the industrials were heavily populating the portfolio, but it didn't last that long. And so my exposure is fairly low now. But I think it will happen again. So I think it's a great sector to focus on for the long term investor waiting for them, the prices to come to them.

DS: Alright. Rob, let's go ahead and leave it there. I really appreciate the look through the sectors here. Today, everyone, can't encourage you enough. Go follow Rob here on Seeking Alpha. He's constantly putting out a wealth of knowledge and research as well as check out his investing group here on Seeking Alpha as well. And, Rob, we'll see you here again hopefully soon in a few episodes. And everyone, we'll see you in the next one.

Get full access to Sungarden YARP Portfolio now!

data.title: Nvidia Stock: A Strong Buy Amidst Tariff Wars (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) has fallen 23% over the last 6 months, down back below $100. It's been a stock I've wanted to initiate a position in for a long time, and I believe we are finally getting there to a position where this stock is undervalued and presents a better opportunity relative to many other stocks.

I first wrote about NVDA in January, initiating a strong sell position which has played out fairly well post the DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) news which opened up some cracks in the bullish thesis such as with margin contraction and added competition. See below how this panned out:

I think at today's prices, NVDA is becoming a buy again, and I'll explain why here.

Investment Thesis

A lot of my investment thesis is based around NVDA's comparison to Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), which essentially shows how strong NVDA is relative to the closest competitor. Here are the numbers:

NVDA net income margin: 56.2%

AMD net income margin: 6.3%

NVDA revenue growth: 77.9%

AMD revenue growth: 24.2%.

The reason NVDA are winning is because they own the full-stack AI. AMD is trying to survive as a hardware company whilst NVDA dominates the full ecosystem with CUDA.

They're also:

  1. Already working on 2027 chips after Blackwell. Hyperscalers are starting to develop their own chips in house and this is of course bearish for NVDA demand, but NVDA's innovation is unmatched. NVDA chips will always be the best, and they aim to create new architecture every year.
  2. NVDA is aiming to become a key player in robotics, which CEO Jensen Huang has said will become the next multi-trillion dollar industry. AMD is nowhere near there.

Tariffs

For the most part, I don't believe tariffs will have a significant impact on NVDA, though of course there will no doubt be some disruption. Here's why we will see disruption:

Increased Production Costs: I'll dive deeper in the Risks section below, but ultimately NVDA will have to onshore, and onshoring will come with increased costs and decreased margins.

Reduced Demand: Depending on how tariffs play out, there will be some countries where buying from NVDA will become far more expensive. This will likely reduce NVDA demand and increase opportunities for competitors to perhaps win some market share from NVDA.

Macroeconomic Impacts: From a macro point of view, there's currently no direction in terms of interest rates, inflation, and general GDP and AI growth. The market has been booming lately but a selloff and general cooling of demand could happen and Trump's tariffs could be the catalyst for this. Personally, I don't see this happening, but it's a potential risk, no doubt.

The above are short-term impacts. Long term, I don't believe we will see a material impact on NVDA over a 5-year time frame as NVDA, along with TSMC (TSM), and building fabs onshore. NVDA has also worked with Foxconn to build onshore advanced data centers, so there's huge investment ahead here. And ultimately, semiconductors are not subject to tariffs currently and if they are, I suspect this will come with much more strategy with a much more gradual plan compared to what we have seen the past few days.

Risks

I believe many of the risks I outlined in my original article still are true, particularly those associated with margin contraction. NVDA's high margins appear to be unsustainable, particularly as competition will likely continue to increase. High margins are obviously a testament to the quality of the business, but this pricing power won't last forever.

As NVDA onshores, we'll likely see some slight margin contraction as labor costs and operational costs are considerably higher (~10%) with TSMC in Arizona vs. Taiwan on a per-wafer basis. These costs will, of course, be passed to NVDA.

On top of this, as onshoring happens, there will inevitably be some supply chain disruption, which could mean NVDA is unable to meet demand in some markets.

The other side to this is that NVDA's margins are way ahead of AMD, Intel (INTC), and other peers by ~20%. And as you'll see in the valuation section below, this makes them still quite undervalued even if we consider some margin contraction.

Valuation

This is the main reason I'm so bullish on NVDA today. Let me first start by comparing NVDA to historical values:

NVDA now trades lower than 2019 Fwd P/E lows at 21.6x. Since 2019, NVDA has:

  • Increased revenues by 1,013%.
  • Increased net income by 1,659%.

And yet, NVDA trades at a similar valuation on a Fwd P/E basis.

We can also look at NVDA on a PEG basis, where it currently trades at 0.61x. Analyst estimates for NVDA in 2027 are $138.4 million, which means NVDA would grow net income at 36.5% CAGR over the next two years. This then means that at a 1x PEG in 2027, NVDA would trade at $208 and at a 1.5x PEG, NVDA would trade at $289.

This would give a 114% and 197% increase respectively, just based on analyst estimates.

Finally, we can look at NVDA's valuation vs. peers, which also makes it appear very attractive. On a Fwd P/E basis, NVDA trades at 21.6x P/E.

Peers trade at the following:

  • INTC: 39.5x P/E
  • Broadcom (AVGO): 22.3x P/E
  • AMD: 17.9x P/E.

Therefore, NVDA trades at the lower end of peers whilst also growing earnings at a much faster rate. For example, AVGO's P/E multiple is higher than NVDA, yet earnings growth is expected to be 29% vs NVDA's 36.5%.

Conclusion

I think NVDA is finally looking very attractive at today's valuation below $100 as I tried to make clear in my valuation section which looked at what a PEG of 1x and 1.5x would give based solely on analyst estimates. The semiconductor industry has some risks with a macro slowdown along with potential tariff effects, but ultimately NVDA still dominates the industry which continues to have very strong tailwinds.

I think a nice dollar cost average into NVDA over the next 12 months, as long as the price remains below $110, will work out very well in the long term.

data.title: RMBS Spreads: Paradox Or Product Of Fed QT And A Frozen Housing Market?
data.description:
This insight seeks to explain the recent divergence in RMBS spreads amid the Fed's quantitative tightening and frozen mortgage issuance. While mortgage originations remain at historic lows due to elevated rates, agency RMBS spreads have widened to near-decade highs.

With US inflation still persistently above the 2% target, and a risk that tariffs cause some deterioration in growth/inflation trade-offs, the US Federal Reserve remains reluctant to cut rates quickly, and left rates unchanged at 4.25% - 4.5%, at the March 19th FOMC meeting. However, the severe equity market sell-off in early-April in response to higher tariffs may presage greater recession risks, which skews risks towards faster easing. Nonetheless, US mortgage rates are still close to multi-year highs at 6.6% as the Chart shows.

30-year fixed mortgage rates of 6.6% mean very few mortgage holders have any incentive to re-finance existing mortgages, as Chart 2 shows for the different mortgage coupons (for all active fixed-rate mortgage pools from the three agencies). On our calculations, only 5.2% of the remaining principal balance for the three agency mortgages has a gross coupon of 7% or above, giving an incentive to refinance with the current mortgage rate at 6.6%. This means mortgage issuance volume remains depressed, since most existing mortgages were taken out with coupons of 2.5-4% as the chart shows.

But despite low mortgage originations and issuance, strong collateral pricing in the housing market, and the agency-guarantee, agency-RMBS option-adjusted spreads versus US Treasuries remain near multi-year highs, as Chart 3 shows (excluding the Covid yield spike). In addition, although IG corporate credit offers less investor protection with no agency guarantee, higher default risks and no collateral, investment grade (IG) credit spreads have converged to the same spread level versus US Treasuries, as agency-RMBS spreads, as Chart 3 also shows.

Further evidence of an apparent anomaly in agency-RMBS spreads is found in the narrowing of non-agency RMBS spreads, like non-Qualifying Mortgages or loans (non-QM) which fully participated in the risk rally since Q4, 2023, with spreads falling steadily versus US Treasuries, as Chart 4 shows. This is consistent with the strong performance of credit generally, and particularly high yield credit. (Non-QM comprise about 15% of the overall non-agency RMBS market, and do not meet the specific underwriting guidelines for an agency guarantee).

So why are agency - MBS spreads near the highs since Covid?

To explain this apparent anomaly, we need to answer the question why did agency-MBS spreads widen so far in 2022-23, after turning negative in 2021? The Fed’s rapid rate increases in 2022-23, its phased reductions in RMBS holdings in the Quantitative Tightening (QT) programme, and the regional banking crisis in March 2023 all contributed to the spread widening.

In addition, the regional banking crisis drove enforced sales of $114bn in agency-MBS, previously owned by the regional banks SVB and SB, as the FDIC, which assumed control of the failed regional banks’ deposits and assets (via a bridging bank) disposed of these assets in a fire sale. This coincided with the Fed’s ongoing QT programme, which began in May 2022, and included the run-down of its agency-MBS holdings at a pace of $17.5bn monthly, rising to $35bn monthly after 3 months.

Largely due to the low prepayment rate on agency-MBS, and zero incentive to refinance mortgages, the Fed has reduced its holdings by considerably less than the planned $35bn monthly since May 2022, but they are still some $510 bn lower than in May 2022, as the Table 1 shows.

Negative convexity and MBS

The pronounced negative convexity of MBS did not help either, in 2022-23, since duration of a bond with negative convexity[2] increases as rates rise. Thus, MBS duration increased as interest rates rose and refinancings began to collapse, as Chart 5 shows. Now however, the negative convexity of 30 yr agency-RMBS is close to zero, as Chart 5 shows, and the Fed has embarked upon a slow easing cycle, so interest rate volatility has fallen sharply, reviving the appeal of RMBS, ceteris paribus.

A slow easing cycle with low interest rate volatility may favour agency-MBS?

It can be argued a slow Fed easing cycle with low-rate volatility is the most favourable for MBS as a fixed income asset. This is because a faster decline in rates could boost mortgage issuance, as new buyers enter the market, and existing mortgage holders increase refinancing, as mortgage rates fall to low enough levels to give an incentive to do so.

…..and even a recession & faster easing cycle may carry greater risks to credit

The combination of a slow easing cycle, RMBS spreads near 10 yr highs versus credit, the likelihood of subdued RMBS issuance and the agency guarantee are all factors suggesting spreads are anomalously high versus high yield credit particularly. Indeed, even if the Fed is forced to accelerate easing because of recession risks, the agency-guarantee should offer strong RMBS support versus credit. It is true that in this scenario, a recession could also impact house prices via increased unemployment, mortgage delinquencies, and repossessions, but note that underwriting standards on mortgages were tightened significantly after the GFC, and US homeowners have much higher levels of equity in housing than in the GFC, as Chart 6 shows. Another risk would be that US banks reduce RMBS holdings further, should loan growth accelerate, and capital adequacy regulations ease.

Chart 6: US house-owners’ equity in real estate

1. Excludes Federal Agency Securities and numbers rounded so doesn’t sum to total.

2. Remember negative convexity of a bond means it falls more in price when yields rise, than rises in price when yields fall.
