data.title: Target: Selling Options Could Yield 14% In This Beaten-Down Value Play
data.description:
Target Corporation (NYSE:TGT) seems to be one of the most hated companies on the market right now.

After slowly losing market cap over the last few years as a result of meager growth and profitability, the stock has come under renewed pressure as of late from the Trump Administration's tariff announcement, which could negatively impact the beleaguered retailer.

With billions in sales and a nationwide presence, the firm has a lot going for it - but slipping margins and an inability to grow the top line have weighed on investor sentiment.

Now, with TGT trading at its lowest valuation in years, we're beginning to sense an opportunity. Sure, the prospects for capital appreciation in TGT remain muted, but we also think that much of the risk premium has been "beaten out" of the stock at this point.

Thus - our trade idea: selling put options on TGT.

A trade like this exchanges potential future appreciation for a direct, up-front cash payout, which appears favorable in the current market environment. While there are risks with a trade like this, it appears to be the most optimal way to generate returns in TGT for the time being.

Today, we'll cover TGT's financial outlook, talk about the company's valuation, and explain why we think a "short put" trade in the stock could be the best way to squeeze returns out of this stable - if somewhat moribund-looking - firm.

Sound good? Let's dive in.

Target's Financials

Let's begin by taking a look at TGT's financials.

In short, the company has seen better days.

It's true that TGT enjoys widespread brand recognition and love (Tar-jay), the company's financial performance leaves a lot to be desired.

On the earnings front, TGT has turned in a number of mediocre-looking quarters, with equal parts earnings misses and beats over the last year or so:

Mixed in with this earnings bumpiness is a categorial lack of any real top-line growth since 2021. As you can see, YoY gains were lackluster in 2022 - in the low single digit range - which were followed by a mix of small gains and losses throughout 2023 and 2024.

Zooming out somewhat, you can see this dynamic in the company's TTM top and bottom-line figures, which have leveled off significantly since 2021:

On the top line, the firm has failed to win new business (and even potentially lost some, when accounting for inflation), and on the bottom line, TGT's margins have shrunk as a result of inflation eating into profitability:

As you can see, gross margins have rebounded somewhat since the fall-off in 2022, but less of this rebound has found its way to the bottom line, which suggests heightened costs of operation, including debt, labor, marketing, etc.

Finally, and from an anecdotal point of view, during recent trips we've taken to Target, we've noticed a distinct lack of care with respect to the firm's cleanliness and presentation. The core goods are still there, but we've found empty boxes strewn about the aisles, empty shelves, and other hiccups that don't match with our existing notion of the company's brand and experience.

So - right now, there are some issues to be addressed.

On the positive side, TGT enjoys strong free cash flows. While the firm has faced issues with growth and profitability, these bumps have not extended to cash flows, where TGT's OCF dwarfs the firm's Capex requirements:

This leaves plenty of cash available for growth investments and distributions to shareholders.

Similarly, looking forward, TGT's management have outlined that they're aware of the issues that TGT faces, and are taking steps to re-invigorate growth in the brand (emphasis added):

Our expectation is to drive more than $15 billion in revenue growth over the next five years...

We are investing in multiple ways to hold and grow share across our merchandising portfolio... new stores and remodels, supply chain enhancements, digital capabilities, newness across our assortment, partnerships with well-loved brands, momentum in loyalty, same-day delivery, our media business and more...

Target Plus is a great example of how we’re taking a fresh look at areas of our business where, we believe, are ripe for growth. By ramping up our investment last year, we have grown Target Plus to a billion-dollar marketplace that’s growing at a double-digit pace. We’re doing it in a way that’s true to Target, giving consumers more choice within the broader umbrella of the trusted Target brand.

We expect that Target Plus - the invite-only vendor marketplace - in combination with inflation and re-investment into store experience (traffic) and efficiencies (margins) should drive better financial results over the next few years.

Analysts are anticipating top-line growth in the lower single-digit range for the rest of the decade, which sounds about right to us:

Net income and EPS growth figures also look similar. All told, we're largely in agreement with these expectations.

Taken together, Target's current position is stable, if somewhat un-energetic, and going forward we see opportunities for improved growth, even if they are down the line somewhat.

Target's Valuation

So - that's the organic business.

How much are you paying for it?

Right now, we see TGT as one of the best 'values' on the market. Sure, the company's growth has been weak, but the top retailer still produces billions in cash flow every year. Why is the stock so inexpensive?

Here's a look at TGT's current multiple situation.

As you can see, Seeking Alpha's quant rating system gives the stock an "A-," which we largely agree with:

There are a couple better values on the market, to be sure, but the company's incredibly low multiple of 5.4x cash flow appears very intriguing.

This price point also represents, to us, that most of the "risk" to investors has been flushed out of the stock price. For evidence of this, look at the company's long-run valuation ranges, and how the current price compares:

On sales, earnings, and book, TGT is trading at extreme lows, which reflects some of the troubles TGT has had when it comes to growing revenues, market share, and profitability.

However, looking at the price action, we see this selloff - particularly following the tariff announcement - as potentially peak pessimism. Have a look at how far TGT's stock is trading below long-run averages:

You can see this dynamic with the bottom columns, which show distance from average pricing measures. As you can see, we're trading outside the standard deviation of that range.

Finally, when using the dividend yield as a valuation yardstick, TGT's current payout sticks out like a sore thumb, showing just how much real yield you're getting when you buy TGT stock right now:

When viewed in sum, we think TGT is a stable - if moribund - retailer, with moderate growth prospects going forward. Trading at an incredibly low, attractive valuation, the stock looks like it may be a long-term value opportunity.

The Trade

Despite that, however, TGT also lacks any short-term catalysts that could spur action-taking at this point. Plus, the news flow around tariffs has been largely negative, which will likely preclude any short-term rebound in the stock price.

What is a sharp trader to do?

We think selling put options is the best course of action for most investors.

When you sell put options, you trade upside in TGT shares in exchange for a firm, up-front cash payout. If the stock goes up or trades sideways, then you keep the cash. If the stock goes down past the option strike price (between now and option expiry), then you need to buy shares in the stock at that price. The good news is that you still get to keep the cash.

For this trade, we like the July 18th, $65 strike options:

These options pay $2.40, or $240 per contract. Given that selling one option requires $6,260 in cash, the yield works out to roughly 3.83% over the next 100 days. This annualizes to roughly 14% in yield.

We like the idea of selling these options because they offer a good blend of yield and safety. The stock needs to drop more than 25% (from here) over the next 100 days for investors to lose money selling them, and in our view, even if they were assigned, they would make a truly spectacular entry point into TGT's longer-term story.

Risks

There are some risks to consider.

First, on the option side of things, if you sell options and the stock drops beneath that strike price by the expiration date, you have to spend the money you reserved on buying shares. Even if the stock were to go to zero overnight, you'd still need to buy shares at $65 per share if you sold these options. It's not a different risk profile than simply buying and holding the stock, but it's something to be aware of. Please carefully review the risks of options trading before trading any options as explained here.

On the business side, getting "net long" TGT at this price comes with challenges. We think the valuation appears extremely attractive, but it could drop further from here. Nothing is guaranteed in the business world, and while TGT has significant inertia, if management continue to stumble on the execution front, then there's further risk to investors.

Additionally, option sellers need to watch earnings announcements carefully, as they can create volatility that sends a stock below a strike price, thus creating short-term unrealized losses.

Summary

Overall, though, we see TGT as a largely stable, deep-value retailer. Sure, the outlook isn't spectacular, but that's why we think selling options on the company makes so much sense. The trade comes with a strong cash yield, and a significantly lower breakeven than buyers at the current price are getting. Net net, it appears to be the most optimal way to play the stock.

From a ratings point of view, all of the information we've presented supports our "Hold" rating. We'd like to upgrade this to a 'Buy', but we're not quite there yet when it comes to news flow or the organic earnings.

Stay safe out there!

data.title: Is Janover The Next Strategy? Not At This Price
data.description:
Earlier this week, micro-cap software company Janover (NASDAQ:JNVR) made waves by announcing a majority stake by a crypto-focused management team that intends to completely shift the company’s business model. The stock closed at $4 the day before the announcement, and then hit a high of $48 before pulling back. Shares are trading at ~$27 as of this writing, so it’s a long way off its highs. This kind of volatility is difficult to trade given the rapid movements, but if we step back and look at the plan, it is my view we’ll see prices a lot lower than $27 in the weeks and months to come. Let’s dig in.

What is Janover?

Janover today is an AI-powered online platform that connects commercial real estate participants to valuable data through a SaaS model. Janover claims to have over 1 million web users annually, which include multifamily and commercial property owners and developers. In addition, it says more than 10% of the financial institutions in America use its platform.

Here’s a basic look at how the company achieves this. It is a data platform for commercial real estate, essentially, touting its use of AI as a way to enhance the value it’s providing to users.

That all sounds nice, but in practice, this business is tiny.

Revenue has been around $2 million annually for a few years, so we’re talking about what would qualify as a small business in the US by any measure.

Janover is building its annual recurring revenue through a subscription model, rather than transactional, and that is working.

The company’s losses are narrowing, but again, we’re talking about a fledgling software business with a tiny revenue base that hasn’t grown for years.

Enter the announcement from earlier this week, and the stock chart looks like this.

The move earlier this week has already been retraced by almost half, so it appears the market is coming to its senses at least a bit. Even after the stock moved ~8X time into Tuesday’s close, it’s still only a $47 million market cap. This thing is extremely small and investors should understand the risks inherent in trading a stock this small. Big gains are certainly possible, but so are big losses. It simply doesn’t take much money moving in or out to move this stock in a big way, so if you’re interested, just keep that in mind.

What happened?

In short, Janover is changing its business model from one of a tiny software provider to that of a digital asset treasury company. If this sounds familiar, it’s because it is; Strategy (MSTR) was the first mover in this space. It also went from a struggling software business to a digital asset treasury company (Bitcoin, of course), and has seen its share price explode higher in the years since. We could argue the merits of such a move, but the returns are undeniable for Strategy shareholders.

It would appear Janover is attempting to do exactly the same thing, except it is focusing on Solana (SOL-USD) rather than Bitcoin. Management states it can earn yield from staking its Solana, but also that Solana is much more volatile than Bitcoin. Management believes this volatility is a source of potential valuation premium for the stock, which I find slightly curious. Generally, investors pay a premium for quality, not volatility. Solana is indeed extremely volatile, which could lead to amplified returns, but also amplified losses. More on that in a bit.

Janover is going to continue to operate its software business, but like Strategy, if the company issues a bunch and/or equity to buy Solana, the core business will be meaningless and nobody will care. That’s what happened with Strategy as the Bitcoin holding business is orders of magnitude larger than the software business. That appears to be the playbook here.

This playbook is almost exactly what Strategy has been doing for years, and Janover is attempting to capture the premium the market has assigned to Strategy over and above its actual Bitcoin holdings, which is significant. The difference is that Janover is using Solana rather than Bitcoin.

Now, as part of the takeover, Janover is going to rename itself to DeFi Development Corporation, and change its ticker at some point in the future. These moves also highlight the idea that the software business Janover currently operates is about to become completely irrelevant, and that the stock is likely to trade with Solana rather than any fundamentals. I wouldn't bother with analyzing the software business any longer.

The company has raised $42 million in an offering of convertible notes to angel investors and private equity. The notes accrue interest at 2.5% per year, paid quarterly, and mature in April 2030. They can be redeemed early so long as the company’s market cap is at least $100 million on the day prior to the conversion date, which is a ~2.5X move from this morning’s price. The proceeds are being used to acquire digital assets, starting with Solana.

What now?

That’s the question, as investors are attempting to revalue this company based upon a complete 180 in terms of its business model. At this point, we can ignore the software business, in my view, as a money-losing business with $2 million of annual revenue simply doesn’t matter in the context of the company holding tens of millions of dollars of cryptocurrency. So let’s instead focus on Solana as a longer-term holding.

Solana has indeed produced some truly eye-popping returns. The bull run that began in late-2023 produced a move from the low-$20s to almost $300. However, that move has also mostly been given up at this point, with Solana at just over $100 at the time of writing. That’s still up huge from the 2023 lows, but enormously painful if you held at $294 and never sold.

That is the obvious risk here with Janover/DevFi; there is no guarantee Solana has to go higher over time. This company is leveraging itself enormously to start, and I’m assuming if it follows the Strategy playbook, even more down the road. If it works, we could see enormous returns for shareholders. If it doesn’t, it has a very good chance of going even lower than the $4 it traded for last Friday.

The bottom line

The strategy that Janover is employing here has worked wonders for Strategy, so I get the logic. Without it, Janover is just a tiny, unprofitable also-ran in the fintech sector. With Solana staking, it could turn into something more. The problem is that even at $27 (or even worse, Tuesday’s close of $33), way too much is being priced in already.

If the company manages to acquire $42 million (give or take) of Solana with basically pure leverage, as it has no appreciable cash on hand to fund anything, the volatility of this stock is going to be epic. Management appears to want that, but it’s something to keep in mind.

Now, the only way this can work is for Solana to go up a bunch after the purchases are made, because otherwise, you just have a business with $42 million of an asset (Solana), and an offsetting $42 million liability (convertible notes). It’s not quite that simple, but that’s essentially how it works. The only way to create value is to see the value of the asset exceed the value of the liability, over and above the cost of the liability. In this case, the cost is 2.5% a year, plus the potential dilution of when they mature.

At $27 per share (or $33), there is simply too much optimism priced in. Prior to the announcement, the stock was worth ~$5.7 million. Today, it’s $47 million. That implies something like $41 million of value created before anything has actually happened. Thus, in order to even justify the current valuation – forgetting about any potential upside – Janover would need its Solana stake to generate ~$41 million of additional value, or roughly double what the company is trying to acquire. No, thanks; I’m at a Strong Sell on this one.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: S&P500: Tactical Buy - The Coiled Spring Trade (Upgrade)
data.description:
The 20% selloff

The S&P500 (SP500) closed below the 5000 level, nearly -20% below the all-time high. Thus, the S&P500 is on the edge of an official bear market.

Let's review the selloff timeline and the triggers - it's all about the tariffs.

  • The first 10% correction since February 21st was due to the soft data in the PMI and ISM surveys which suggested that businesses and consumers were getting concerned that tariffs would cause lower growth and higher inflation.
  • The tariffs were announced on April 2nd - and before the announcement and after the first indication that tariffs could be limited only to 10% universal tariffs, the stock markets were rebounding from the 10% correction, pushing on the critical 200dma resistance.
  • However, the tariffs announced were worse than the worst-case scenario, focusing on eliminating trade deficit with punitive tariffs on each trading partner, in addition to the minimum 10% tariff.
  • Thus, the next 5% selloff on Thursday was in response to the punitive tariffs - the day after the announcement.
  • The subsequent 5% selloff was due to the China's retaliatory tariffs on Friday.
  • Many expected that if there were no indications of trade de-escalation over the weekend, the markets could crash on Monday. There were no de-escalations, and the S&P500 was lower by over 5% in the futures market overnight, and opened lower.
  • However, during the trading day on Monday, there was a false rumor that the US would delay te tariffs by 90 days - and this sent stocks soaring with nearly a 10% intraday swing from the bottom to the top. This was the so-called coiled spring bounce, which faded. However, the S&P500 finished nearly flat for the day.
  • The action on Monday tells us that slightly positive news on trade could send the stock market soaring higher.
  • In fact, the Monday's positive action continued on Tuesday, with the S&P500 up over 4%, mostly due to the trade negotiations between Japan and the US. This confirms that if the current trade war evolves into negotiations with eventual tariff reductions, the stock market could soar higher.
  • The Tuesday rally was "killed" with the US announcing additional 50% tariff on China due to China's retaliatory tariff - this was the third selloff. However, this selloff was more muted, and the S&P500 closed above Monday's intraday lows, and above the bear market threshold. In addition, VIX (VXX) closed below Monday's high.
  • Thus, the question is whether Monday's low will hold as the bottom over the near term?

Here is the S&P500 chart showing the unfolding "crash", and the well-defined range 4800-5250.

Here is the VIX chart showing that peak fear was possibly on Monday.

What's next?

From this point the S&P500 will either enter a bear market with the selloff in excess of 20%, or we will get a major bounce to at least the 5500 level, and possibly higher.

The bearish case

The bear market at this point will have to be triggered by the data suggesting an imminent recession. The recent March labor market report does not suggest that we were in a recession in March, but this was before the tariffs were implemented. Thus, in my opinion, the current selloff will continue once we get the evidence that the labor market is slowing, which makes the weekly claims data released each Thursday extremely important. At this point, the weekly claims do not support the recessionary bear market.

In addition, the corporate earnings have to contract to justify the further selloff, and the Q1 earnings season is unlikely to show a contraction in earnings. However, corporate guidance will be important if corporations significantly slash their projected revenues. The upcoming earnings season over the next few weeks will be important.

The bullish case

This entire selloff was triggered by the tariffs, and President Trump is in full control - meaning he can stop this selloff at any point, and trigger a massive rally.

It is unreasonable to expect that President Trump would trigger a recessionary bear market, which could lead to the housing bubble crash and consequently cause a real financial crisis.

Thus, given that the selloff in excess of 20% would be dangerous for the real economy, it reasonable to assume that the 20% threshold could be the near-term bottom. This might be the level at which the Trump put is activated.

The fact that Tuesday's selloff was relatively modest and the S&P500 closed above the Monday's intraday lows is positive - given the horrible news of 104% tariffs on China.

In fact, the 104% tariff on China might be the worst news in this trade war cycle. In my opinion, we are transitioning to the negotiations stage, and we will get some positive news from these negotiations, especially with Japan and South Korea. Given the China 104% tariff, the EU is unlikely to retaliate, which is also a positive news.

Implications

I'm going tactically Long on the S&P500 (SPX), after covering the short position.

What does this mean? For me, tactically Long means I expect a sharp rally from the 20% threshold level. In fact, the rally could be so sharp, that the S&P500 could bounce by 10% in one day - it could spike to the 5500 level in a day. That's the coiled spring bounce - and the market is ready for it.

Specifically, there are many shorts, like the trend-followers CTA who will be squeezed higher and forced to cover, and there are still FOMO traders who can't wait to jump into the market.

One man controls the market and many of his associates are begging for de-escalation. The fact that Elon Musk is publicly confronting Navarro is a positive sign and signals a possible de-escalation.

Now, being tactically Long is a trade, not an investment. That means it's relatively small, and it has a strict stop-loss in case it goes the other way. I still think we will have a recessionary bear market with the AI bubble bust, and a credit event, so I would not buy yet for long-term at these levels, given the still elevated valuations and uncertain earnings growth.
